ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic dumpling class; no specific Chinese chive and egg dumpling entry exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers dumplings as a category; no specific chive-and-egg variant exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Chinese chive and egg dumplings; SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual dish variants at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chinese chives and egg dumplings is approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 150 g, commonly consumed as a main dish at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Chives And Egg Dumplings — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact dish 'Chinese Chives And Egg Dumplings' (record G00010077). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical source at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese chives and egg dumplings. GI estimated at approximately 48 (low) based on the closest analogous food: wheat-flour dumplings / filled pasta (GI ~46–50) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The wheat wrapper is the primary glycemic driver; the egg and chive filling has negligible glycemic impact. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (48) × carbohydrate per serving (8.03 g/100g × 150 g serving = 12.05 g) / 100 = 5.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content at 46 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Chinese chives and egg dumplings are suitable for individuals with hypertension due to their very low sodium content (46 mg/100 g); however, patients should avoid high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or chili sauce, which can substantially increase the sodium load of the meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-moderate carbohydrate (8 g/100 g), negligible sugar (0.21 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~48)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chinese chives and egg dumplings have a low estimated glycemic index (~48) and low sugar content, making them appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; the egg protein content further moderates postprandial glucose response, but patients should avoid sweet dipping sauces and limit portion size to control total carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1 g/100 g), low sodium (46 mg/100 g), and moderate cholesterol (28 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chinese chives and egg dumplings present a low cardiovascular risk profile with low saturated fat and sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer steamed or boiled preparation over pan-frying to minimise added oil and advanced glycation end-product formation, and should avoid high-sodium or high-fat dipping sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory organosulfur compounds in Chinese chives; no red meat or trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chinese chives contain organosulfur compounds with anti-inflammatory properties, and eggs provide vitamin D; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer steamed or boiled preparation to minimise AGE formation associated with pan-frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Chives and Egg Dumplings (韭菜鸡蛋饺子, jiǔcài jīdàn jiǎozi) are a traditional Chinese dumpling variety widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The filling consists of finely chopped Chinese chives (jiucai, Allium tuberosum) combined with scrambled or beaten egg, seasoned with sesame oil and salt, and enclosed in a thin wheat-flour wrapper. The dumplings are typically boiled, steamed, or pan-fried (guotie style). The texture is soft and yielding when boiled or steamed, with a lightly crisp base when pan-fried. The flavour is savoury and mildly garlicky, with the characteristic pungent-sweet aroma of Chinese chives complemented by the richness of egg. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 101 kcal, 8.71 g protein, 3.78 g fat, and 8.03 g carbohydrate, with very low sodium (46 mg) and negligible sugar (0.21 g). The estimated glycemic index is approximately 48 (low), making this dish suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is a good source of iron (1.79 mg/100 g) and provides moderate potassium (82 mg/100 g). Clinically, the low sodium content makes it appropriate for hypertensive individuals, the low GI and moderate protein content support glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes, and the low saturated fat and sodium profile is favourable for cardiovascular health. Chinese chives are rich in antioxidants and organosulfur compounds with anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and dairy-free, but contains gluten and egg allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact dish at confidence 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (wheat-flour dumplings) and is documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.0 g/100 g based on the known fat composition of egg and minimal oil in a comparable chive-and-egg dumpling from USDA FoodData Central (closest analogous entry); this is documented as an adapted estimate. dietary_fibre_g estimated at 0.8 g/100 g based on the chive and wheat wrapper content using USDA FoodData Central analogous data; HPB does not report fibre for this record. GI is an analogous estimate (~48, low) based on wheat-flour dumplings from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish has been published. All core macros (protein_g 8.71, fat_g 3.78, carbohydrate_g 8.03) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
